painScales.name: Visual Analog Scale (VAS)
painScales.description: 0 (no pain) to 10 (worst imaginable pain), captured during paroxysms

painScales.name: Barrow Neurological Institute Pain Intensity Score
painScales.description: Grade I (no pain, no medication) to V (severe pain, no relief)

severityClassification.description: Recurrent paroxysms of unilateral facial pain in trigeminal distribution, triggered by innocuous stimuli, with MRI evidence of neurovascular compression
Same clinical features but no neurovascular compression identified on MRI
Trigeminal neuralgia due to underlying structural lesion (tumor, MS plaque, AVM)

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Sudden, recurrent, electric shock-like, stabbing, or shooting unilateral facial pain lasting seconds to two minutes
painDistribution: Multiple divisions may be involved; bilateral in 3-5% of cases (more common in MS-associated TN)
triggerFactors: Light touch on face (most common trigger)
Chewing, brushing teeth, shaving, applying makeup
Cold wind or air current on face
Trigger zones: specific areas where light touch reliably triggers pain, often in central face
characteristics: Electric shock-like, stabbing, shooting, or lancinating quality
Paroxysmal, each burst lasting seconds to up to 2 minutes
Refractory period after each paroxysm during which triggers are ineffective
No persistent background pain between paroxysms (classical TN; if persistent pain, consider atypical TN or trigeminal neuropathic pain)
naturalHistory: Episodic pattern with spontaneous remissions lasting weeks to months or years
Progressive increase in attack frequency and severity over time
Remissions tend to shorten as disease progresses

neurologicExam: Normal neurologic examination in classical or idiopathic trigeminal neuralgia
Sensory examination is normal to light touch, pinprick, temperature in trigeminal distribution
Facial motor function normal (motor root of trigeminal: muscles of mastication)
triggerZoneIdentification: Gently touching specific areas of face may reproduce characteristic pain
Common trigger zones: nasolabial fold, ala nasi, upper lip, lower lip, chin, eyebrow
Trigger zones may be in same or different trigeminal division from pain radiation
redFlagFindings: Sensory loss or hypoesthesia in trigeminal distribution (suggests secondary TN from mass lesion or MS)
Diminished corneal reflex (suggests V1 involvement from structural lesion)
Trigeminal motor weakness (jaw deviation on opening, weak masseter/temporalis)
Atypical features: bilateral involvement, younger age at onset (<40 years), associated headache

riskFactors: Female sex: female to male ratio approximately 2:1
Hypertension: associated with increased risk of neurovascular compression
Multiple sclerosis: 1-2% of acute MS exacerbations manifest as TN; MS patients have 20x increased risk
Arterial tortuosity and elongation with aging promotes neurovascular contact with trigeminal nerve root entry zone
Familial cases: rare but reported, autosomal dominant inheritance pattern suggested in some pedigrees
Anatomical variants: posterior fossa vascular anomalies, persistent trigeminal artery

subtypes.name: Classical Trigeminal Neuralgia
subtypes.description: Neurovascular compression of the trigeminal nerve root entry zone by a blood vessel (most commonly superior cerebellar artery)
subtypes.details: MRI demonstrates neurovascular contact with morphological changes in the trigeminal nerve root; normal neurologic examination; excellent response to carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine

subtypes.name: Idiopathic Trigeminal Neuralgia
subtypes.description: No identifiable cause despite adequate investigation
subtypes.details: No neurovascular compression on high-resolution MRI; similar clinical features to classical TN; response to medications may be less robust

subtypes.name: Secondary Trigeminal Neuralgia
subtypes.description: Due to an underlying structural cause other than neurovascular compression
subtypes.details: Causes include: MS plaque (most common secondary cause), cerebellopontine angle tumors (schwannoma, meningioma, epidermoid), arteriovenous malformations, saccular aneurysms, brainstem infarction, Chiari malformation; often has sensory abnormalities on examination

subtypes.name: Trigeminal Neuropathic Pain
subtypes.description: May mimic TN but different pathophysiology: constant pain from nerve injury (not paroxysmal; no refractory period)
subtypes.details: Causes: post-herpetic neuralgia, post-surgical/traumatic trigeminal neuropathy, dental causes; constant burning or aching pain with superimposed paroxysms; sensory loss present

mimics: Temporomandibular joint disorder: pain with jaw movement, local tenderness, constant dull ache
Dental pain or abscess: continuous throbbing pain, localized to tooth, worse with cold/hot, radiographic findings
Cluster headache: autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, conjunctival injection), circadian periodicity
Paroxysmal hemicrania: shorter duration than cluster, absolute response to indomethacin
Short-lasting unilateral neuralgiform headache attacks (SUNCT/SUNA): more trigemino-autonomic features, longer attacks
Glossopharyngeal neuralgia: pain in throat, ear, tonsillar fossa, triggered by swallowing (distinct distribution)
Post-herpetic neuralgia: history of VZV eruption, constant burning pain, allodynia, scarring
Persistent idiopathic facial pain (atypical facial pain): continuous dull ache, bilateral, no triggers, no response to carbamazepine
Giant cell arteritis: temporal headache, jaw claudication, elevated ESR/CRP, age >50
Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias: autonomic features with headache, specific therapeutic responses

bedside: Detailed neurologic examination including all cranial nerves
Sensory testing: light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration in all three trigeminal divisions
Motor trigeminal testing: jaw opening/deviation, masseter and temporalis bulk and strength
Jaw jerk reflex: may be brisk in pseudobulbar palsy or UMN lesions
laboratory: Routine blood work generally not diagnostic
MRI with contrast is the primary investigation; laboratory studies are adjunctive
ESR and CRP: if giant cell arteritis suspected in older patients
Viral serologies and CSF analysis: if infectious or inflammatory cause suspected
Autoimmune serologies: if multiple sclerosis suspected (may include CSF oligoclonal bands, IgG index)

imaging.modality: MRI brain with high-resolution trigeminal nerve protocol
imaging.details: Essential for all patients with suspected trigeminal neuralgia to exclude secondary causes
findings: Neurovascular compression of trigeminal nerve root entry zone: most commonly superior cerebellar artery (SCA), anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), or pontine vein
Trigeminal nerve atrophy or displacement at compression site
Cerebellopontine angle tumors: schwannoma, meningioma, epidermoid cyst
Multiple sclerosis: demyelinating plaque affecting trigeminal nerve root entry zone in pons
Arteriovenous malformations, aneurysms, other vascular lesions

imaging.details: Heavily T2-weighted sequences for optimal visualization of cisternal trigeminal nerve and relationship to adjacent vessels
imaging.findings: Demonstrates trigeminal nerve course through prepontine cistern; identifies vessel indenting nerve root entry zone; distinguishes vessel from nerve

imaging.details: Time-of-flight MR angiography to differentiate arterial from venous compression
imaging.findings: Identifies specific compressing vessel (artery vs vein); assesses for vascular malformations or aneurysms

imaging.modality: CT head with contrast (if MRI contraindicated)
imaging.details: Less sensitive than MRI for neurovascular compression and MS plaques
imaging.findings: May identify large tumors, vascular malformations, or significant bony pathology

imaging.details: Invasive, largely replaced by high-resolution MRI
imaging.findings: May demonstrate trigeminal nerve anatomy and compression in cases where MRI is inconclusive

management.protocolReference: European Academy of Neurology guideline on trigeminal neuralgia (Bendtsen et al., European Journal of Neurology 2019)

firstLine.dose: Start 100-200 mg twice daily, titrate by 100 mg every 3-7 days to response; typical maintenance 400-800 mg daily in divided doses; maximum 1200 mg daily
firstLine.mechanism: Sodium channel blocker, stabilizes neuronal membranes, reduces ectopic discharge
firstLine.monitoring: CBC, LFTs, serum sodium, renal function at baseline and periodically; serum levels 4-12 mcg/mL therapeutic range
firstLine.adverseEffects: Dizziness, drowsiness, ataxia, hyponatremia, leukopenia, rash (including SJS/TEN), hepatotoxicity, drug interactions (CYP3A4 inducer)

firstLine.dose: Start 150-300 mg twice daily, titrate by 300 mg every 3-7 days; typical maintenance 600-1200 mg daily in divided doses; maximum 1800 mg daily
firstLine.mechanism: Sodium channel blocker, similar to carbamazepine but better tolerated
firstLine.monitoring: Serum sodium at baseline and periodically (higher risk of hyponatremia than carbamazepine)
firstLine.adverseEffects: Hyponatremia (more common than carbamazepine), dizziness, drowsiness, diplopia; better GI tolerance; no hepatic auto-induction; less drug interaction

secondLine.dose: Start 25 mg daily, slow titration to 200-400 mg daily divided twice daily
secondLine.monitoring: Watch for rash including SJS/TEN (slow titration required)

secondLine.dose: Start 300 mg daily, titrate to 900-3600 mg daily divided three times daily
secondLine.adverseEffects: Dizziness, sedation, peripheral edema, weight gain

secondLine.dose: Start 75 mg twice daily, increase to 150-600 mg daily divided twice daily
Start 5-10 mg three times daily, titrate to 50-80 mg daily

thirdLine: Topiramate: 50-400 mg daily divided twice daily, side effects: word-finding difficulty, metabolic acidosis, nephrolithiasis
Valproic acid: 500-2000 mg daily, side effects: tremor, weight gain, hepatotoxicity, teratogenicity
Phenytoin: rarely used now, narrow therapeutic window, drug interactions

microvascularDecompression.description: Open neurosurgical procedure via retrosigmoid craniotomy; placing a Teflon felt pad between offending vessel and trigeminal nerve root entry zone
microvascularDecompression.indications: Classical TN with neurovascular compression on MRI, refractory to medication, good surgical candidate
microvascularDecompression.success: Long-term pain relief in 70-80% at 10 years; 90% immediate success rate
microvascularDecompression.risks: Hearing loss (5%), CSF leak, infection, facial numbness, diplopia, brainstem injury (rare), stroke (rare)
microvascularDecompression.idealFor: Younger, healthy patients; patients with clear arterial compression; those desiring non-lesional procedure

trigeminalNerveBlock.description: Peripheral nerve block with local anesthetic and steroid at trigger zone or gasserian ganglion
trigeminalNerveBlock.techniques: Supraorbital, infraorbital, or mental nerve block; gasserian ganglion block
trigeminalNerveBlock.indications: Diagnostic confirmation, short-term relief while awaiting surgery or medication titration
trigeminalNerveBlock.success: Temporary relief lasting weeks to months
trigeminalNerveBlock.limitedBy: Temporary effect; not definitive long-term treatment

percutaneousLesioningProcedures.description: Heat lesion of gasserian ganglion via percutaneous needle through foramen ovale under fluoroscopic guidance
percutaneousLesioningProcedures.success: 90% initial pain relief; 50% recurrence at 5 years
percutaneousLesioningProcedures.risks: Facial numbness (universal), anesthesia dolorosa (rare), corneal numbness, masseter weakness

percutaneousLesioningProcedures.description: Compression of gasserian ganglion with Fogarty balloon catheter
percutaneousLesioningProcedures.risks: Facial numbness, masseter weakness (more common than RF), bradycardia during inflation

percutaneousLesioningProcedures.description: Injection of anhydrous glycerol into gasserian ganglion
percutaneousLesioningProcedures.success: Slightly lower long-term efficacy than RF

stereotacticRadiosurgery.description: Gamma Knife or CyberKnife focused radiation targeting trigeminal nerve root entry zone
stereotacticRadiosurgery.indications: Failed prior procedures, coagulopathy, high surgical risk, recurrent TN
stereotacticRadiosurgery.success: 60-70% initial pain relief; delayed onset (weeks to months); 50% maintain relief at 5 years
stereotacticRadiosurgery.risks: Facial numbness (10-15%), rare sensory loss; no surgical risks; delayed radiation effects possible

pathways: First-line: carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine monotherapy with slow titration
If inadequate response/intolerance: switch to alternative first-line or add second-line agent (lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin)
If medical refractory (failed 2-3 medications): MRI with trigeminal nerve protocol to assess for neurovascular compression
If neurovascular compression: offer microvascular decompression (MVD) for good surgical candidates
If no compression or not surgical candidate: consider percutaneous lesioning procedure or stereotactic radiosurgery
If secondary TN: treat underlying cause (MS: MS-DMT; tumor: surgical resection; AVM: embolization)

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Ectopic action potential generation and ephaptic transmission at sites of trigeminal nerve demyelination
neurovascularCompression: Pulsatile compression by aberrant blood vessel (usually SCA) at trigeminal nerve root entry zone
Chronic compression causes focal demyelination of trigeminal nerve root
Demyelinated axons become hyperexcitable, generate spontaneous ectopic impulses
Adjacent demyelinated axons develop ephaptic cross-talk: touch signals in Aβ fibers are transmitted to adjacent Aδ and C nociceptive fibers
centralMechanisms: Loss of central inhibition: damage to trigeminal afferents reduces central inhibitory control
Hyperexcitability of trigeminal nucleus caudalis (medullary dorsal horn)
Abnormal after-discharges and wind-up in central trigeminal pathways
Reduced efficacy of descending pain modulatory pathways
pathophysiology.triggerZoneMechanism: Light touch A-beta afferents from trigger zones activate low-threshold mechanoreceptors that, due to ephaptic transmission, aberrantly activate nociceptive pathways producing pain paroxysms
pathophysiology.refractoryPeriod: After a paroxysm, axons are refractory due to prolonged hyperpolarization; explains why triggering is impossible during this period
secondaryTNPathophysiology: MS: demyelinating plaque at trigeminal root entry zone in the pons
Tumor: direct compression or infiltration of trigeminal nerve
AVM: vascular compression or ischemic effects

complications: Severe paroxysmal pain leading to significant functional impairment and disability
Weight loss and malnutrition from inability to chew or swallow without triggering pain
Inability to maintain oral hygiene leading to dental complications
Social isolation, anxiety, depression, and suicidal ideation (known as the 'suicide disease')

Adverse effects of carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine: hyponatremia (can cause seizures if severe), bone marrow suppression, hepatotoxicity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome (HLA-B*1502 associated in Asian populations)
mustNotMiss: All patients with suspected trigeminal neuralgia require MRI with trigeminal nerve protocol to exclude secondary causes (tumor, MS, AVM)
Sensory loss on examination is NOT part of classical TN - suggests secondary cause
Bilateral facial pain is unusual for classical TN and suggests MS-related TN
Check HLA-B*1502 in Asian patients before starting carbamazepine to assess SJS/TEN risk
Monitor serum sodium 2-4 weeks after starting oxcarbazepine or carbamazepine (higher risk with oxcarbazepine)
If pain is continuous (not purely paroxysmal), consider atypical trigeminal neuralgia or trigeminal neuropathic pain
emergencyPriority: MODERATE - Pain is severe but not life-threatening; urgent specialist referral indicated for refractory pain
diagnosticClues: Electric shock-like paroxysms lasting seconds to 2 minutes, unilateral in trigeminal distribution
Triggered by innocuous tactile stimuli (light touch, eating, talking)
Trigger zones in central face (nasolabial fold, upper lip, chin)
Complete or partial response to carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine (often considered diagnostic)
Refractory period after each paroxysm prevents continuous triggering
Abrupt onset and offset of pain paroxysms
Neurovascular compression of trigeminal nerve on high-resolution MRI
exclusionClues: Continuous pain without paroxysms suggests atypical facial pain or neuropathic pain
Burning or aching pain quality rather than electric shocks suggests trigeminal neuropathic pain
Pain with autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, conjunctival injection) suggests trigeminal autonomic cephalalgia
Pain triggered by non-facial stimuli (e.g., neck movement, head position) suggests cervical pathology
Abnormal trigeminal sensation on examination suggests secondary TN
Age <40 years without family history warrants detailed search for secondary cause
Poor response to adequate dose of carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine should prompt reconsideration of diagnosis and MRI review
